other_observers: Riley Morris, Amy Dobbins, Jenny Winegardner, Jan Rees, John Rees, John Bregar, Dugald Owen, Jo Dobbins, Heather Morris, and Leland Flores; Riley Morris found the bird on 5/20/10.
specific_location: The bird was seen along the Animas River Trail in Durango, behind the Four Corners River Sports building. The section of the trail is just east of US 160, about 0.5 mile south of Santa Rita Drive.
description: The bird was identified as a warbler due to it’s small size, the bill shape (pointed), and the foraging behavior.   Similar in size to Orange-crowned Warblers in the vicinity.  Details:  The crown was yellow. The face was very distinctly patterned. A dark gray auricular patch contrasted with a thin, white supercilium and broad, white malar region.  The chin and throat were a slightly lighter gray than the auricular patch. The bill was very pointed.  The upperparts were light gray – similar in color to the chin and throat. The wings showed an upper and lower wing bar. Both wing bars were yellow. (The lower wing bar was very broad while the upper wing bar was conspicuous but narrower. Essentially, the 2 wing bars on each wing appeared as a patch with a slight interruption in it.)  Tail was about average in length for a warbler. The color of the tail was gray above and mostly white from below. When the bird fanned her tail, the outer tail feathers showed extensive white with a thin gray outer edge.  The underparts were mostly light gray, becoming whiter at the belly.Behaviors: The bird was extremely active. She would often hang from the foliage of the cottonwoods and box elder trees while gleaning insects. At times, she would drop a few feet before rising straight up a similar distance. She probed for small caterpillar-like prey from dead leaves.Call: A very high- pitched chip (given in flight) would sometimes help with locating her.Plumage: adult
